heading: OPERARE IN TUTTE E SU TUTTE ESERCITARE IL POTERE
card_summary: This long section of the Cantico Terzo, Tono Quarto, treats the human capacity for universal operation and prophetic power. Zorzi opens with the Hermetic axiom (Trismegistus) that God made the world and man as two images of himself, then catalogues occult sympathies — magnetic, mineral, animal, and astral — drawing on Pliny, Cicero, Democritus, and Pythagoras. He argues that man, as the vinculum of all things and uniquely suffused with divinity, can operate upon all things once he governs himself. The section develops through a classification of true prophecy: dreams (Matthew, Job, Pharaoh, Nebuchadnezzar), hypnagogic visions (Ambrose, Lucian the priest), and divine furor (Plato's Ion and Phaedrus, Saul's prophetic ecstasy, Caiphas), culminating in a survey of ecstasy from Plotinus, Pythagoras, and Zoroaster through Francis of Assisi, Augustine, Thomas Aquinas, and Mary of Egypt. The Kabbalistic term bat qol ("daughter of the voice") is introduced as the Hebrew name for all forms of reflected divine speech. Sources include Hermes Trismegistus, Cicero's De divinatione, Plato, Aristotle's Meteorologica and Nicomachean Ethics, Proclus, Porphyry, and Ptolemy's Centiloquium.
essay:
# OPERARE IN TUTTE E SU TUTTE ESERCITARE IL POTERE
## (Cantico Terzo, Tono Quarto, pp. 2207–2280)

*Note: This summary is based on the opening portion of this very long section (approx. the first 6,000 words, covering Chapters 1–4 of the section). The later chapters (covering pp. 2207–2280 in full) are not represented.*

---

## Chapter 1: The Occult Sympathy of All Things

Zorzi opens with the Hermetic cosmological axiom drawn from Trismegistus: God, the supremely overflowing Artisan ("Artefice sommamente sovrabbondante"), created two images of himself — the world and man — in order to delight in the one and enjoy every pleasure in the other. This dual-image theology sets the anthropological foundation for the entire section: man is the microcosm, and his power of operation upon all things derives from this structural identity with the cosmos.

To substantiate the doctrine of occult sympathy (*sympatheia*), Zorzi assembles a dense catalogue of natural wonders: the magnet's power over iron; amber and balas ruby attracting straw when rubbed; the *aetite* stone strengthening unborn children when placed above pregnant women; jasper stopping haemorrhages; the *echeneis* (remora) halting ships; coral improving the humour; the hyacinth stone strengthening travellers; rhubarb purging bile. He notes that no natural philosopher has been able to explain the *cause* of any of these phenomena.

> «A tutti è noto che nella calamita è presente una potenza con la quale essa attrae il ferro e, analogamente, l'ambra e il balagio, se riscaldati per sfregamento sono in grado di spostare una pagliuzza. Tuttavia finora nessuno di quelli che riempiono ponderosi volumi con le loro trattazioni dedicate alle proprietà naturali è riuscito a dire da donde venga o che cosa sia.»

<cite>p. 2207 / fol. XXXVIIIr</cite>

Zorzi then expands the catalogue to animal divination: roosters who know the hours; birds predicting rain; dolphins forecasting storms; Cicero's account of roosters presaging the Boeotian victory over Sparta; and various prodigies from classical and contemporary history (the fall of Byzantium, portents for Venice, Bologna, and Modena). He aligns this doctrine of cosmic sympathy with the Greek term *sympathia*, arguing that all such occult correspondences find their highest expression in *man*, who is "il vincolo di tutte le cose e tutte le abbraccia" — the bond of all things who embraces them all.

The section concludes with the programmatic turn: if all things act upon each other by hidden virtue, it follows that man, as the image and symbol of everything, can act upon everything, provided he governs himself and preserves God within him.

---

## Chapter 2: Man as the Most Perfect Bond — Divine Indwelling and Universal Dominion

Zorzi's central claim is that the self-governing man is inhabited and assisted by God who "works all things" ("Deus omnia operans"). His witnesses here span pagan and Christian sources with characteristic Zorzian catholicity:

- Plato in the *Ion* and *Parmenides*
- Hermes Trismegistus in the *Poimandres* (addressed to Tat)
- Xystus the Pythagorean: "man is the temple of God, God is his guest"
- Ovid (*Fasti*): "Est Deus in nobis, sunt et commercia coeli"
- Paul: "Omnia possum in eo qui me confortat" (Phil 4:13)
- Aristotle (*Meteorologica* and end of the *Nicomachean Ethics*): no virtue, natural or moral, exists except through God

> «Quanto più abbandoniamo questa vita animale e umana, tanto più vivremo la vita degli angeli e di Dio. Uniti a essi e restituiti al nostro destino migliore, noi avremo potere su tutte le cose perché su tutte domineremo.»

<cite>p. 2046</cite>

Zorzi interprets the Genesis command "Fructificate, et multiplicate, et replete terram, et summittite eam, et dominamini" (Gen 1:28) allegorically: "fructify" means good works; "fill the earth" means flooding even the earthly body with divinity; "dominate fish, birds, and beasts" signifies spiritual dominion over the three elemental realms — aqueous and terrestrial powers, including infernal ones. The restoration of man to his prelapsarian dignity is the precondition for this universal dominion. Before the Fall, man held that honour but did not understand it; concupiscence made him like the senseless beasts and, in the Hermetic formula, "da signore divenne il più miserabile degli schiavi."

---

## Chapter 3: The Well-Harmonised Man as Prophet

Building on the cosmic sympathy argument, Zorzi turns to *prophecy* as the primary modality of human power over all things. The chapter opens with Isaiah (Is 41:23): "Annunciateci il futuro e diremo che siete dei" — foreknowledge is the distinctive prerogative of God, shared with those who are joined to him. Peter (2 Pet 1:21) confirms: "Non da volontà umana fu recata mai una profezia, ma mossi dallo Spirito santo parlarono i santi uomini di Dio."

Zorzi then assembles pagan testimony: the Stoics (via Cicero, *De divinatione* I), Ptolemy's *Centiloquium* ("Solo se ispirati dal cielo possono predire in dettaglio le vicende future"), Cratippus, Zeno, Pythagoras, and a gallery of ancient prophets — Calanus the Indian predicting Alexander's death, the Persian Magi, Socrates predicting Crito's death, Diotima predicting the plague, Anaximander warning Sparta of the earthquake, Epimenides prophesying ten years before the Persian Wars.

He then surveys the major *types* of divinatory practice: astrology, augury, haruspicy, pyromancy, hydromancy, geomancy, necromancy (distinguished sharply into those who submit to impure intelligences in idolatry, and those who compel them by the power of divine names), chiromancy (based on Job 37:7: "posuit in manibus hominum ut inde noscat unusquisque opera sua"), and physiognomy. All, Zorzi argues, reduce to the primary genera described; the final section focuses exclusively on legitimate prophecy.

---

## Chapter 4: The True Prophet — Dreams, Hypnagogic Vision, and Divine Furor

Zorzi provides a systematic classification of genuine prophecy under four modalities, uniting them under the Kabbalistic term *bat qol*:

> «Tutti questi modi sono riconducibili a un solo genere di divinazione che gli ebrei chiamano בת קול, bat qol, ossia 'figlia della voce', mentre noi chiamiamo il fenomeno 'eco', cioè una riflessione della voce.»

<cite>p. 2054 / fol. XLIr</cite>

**1. Dreams (*per somnium*):** Matthew on the angel appearing to Joseph; Pharaoh's dream (interpreted by Joseph); Nebuchadnezzar's dream of the succession of kingdoms (interpreted by Daniel); Job on nocturnal visions (Job 4:13ff.).

**2. Hypnagogic vision (*inter somnum et vigiliam*):** Ambrose's account of his own vision of Gervasius and Protasius "with eyes open, neither fully awake nor fully asleep"; the vision of Lucian the priest to whom Gamaliel appeared, indicating the burial place of Stephen.

**3. Divine furor (*per furorem*):** Democritus on poets who sang things neither nature nor art had taught them; Plato on Timnichus of Chalcis composing the most beautiful hymn to Apollo in a state of possession (*Ion*); the *Phaedrus* on poetry's doors being struck in vain without furor. Zorzi adopts Plato's definition: "L'invasamento è astrazione, alienazione e legame" — abstraction from shadowy appearances, alienation from the animal life, and bond to the divine.

Biblical exemplars: Saul's ecstasy ("insiluit spiritus Domini in Saul et prophetavit," 1 Sam 10:10), and Caiphas who "prophetavit cum esset pontifex anni illius." Cicero's *De divinatione* is quoted directly on the second genus of divination through excited or liberated mental motion.

> «Inest namque animi praesagatio extrinsecus iniecta, atque inclusa divinitus. Ea si exarsit acrius, furor appellatur, cum a corpore abstractus animus, divino furore concitatur, ut Democritus et Plato asserunt.»

<cite>p. 2058–2059 (Cicero quoted by Zorzi)</cite>

Zorzi follows this with an extensive catalogue of *ecstasy* (*excessus mentis, extasis*): Porphyry's account of Plotinus being four times released from the body and himself experiencing the vision once at age 68; Heraclitus and Democritus seeking solitude; Pythagoras hidden ten years and Zoroaster twenty; Hermotimus of Clazomenae whose soul wandered; Rabbi Shem Tov ben Falaqera's report that Aristotle in the book *On Bdellium* claimed his own soul was elevated to contemplation of the highest things; Archimedes oblivious to the sack of Syracuse; Cornelius the priest at Padua witnessing remotely the battle of Pharsalus.

The Christian gallery includes: Francis of Assisi carried outside himself passing through Borgo San Sepolcro; Augustine absorbed in meditation not noticing the woman consulting him; Thomas Aquinas levitated a cubit from the ground; Mary Magdalene lifted by angels at fixed hours in the solitude near Marseilles; Mary of Egypt levitated a cubit in prayer; and Clare of Assisi lying from Holy Thursday to Easter Sunday unaware of the passage of time. Zorzi adds a first-person witness: "comprobavi in altera, nomine et vita consimili" — he claims to have personally verified a comparable case.

**4. Clear vision (*per claram visionem*):** Introduced at the close of Chapter 4 drawing on Proclus: the purified mind feasts on truth in pure splendour, initiated into "perfecta, stabiliaque et sincera phantasmata" — not images extracted from the senses but those higher archetypes the prophets contemplated before purification through sacrifice and the purgatorial virtues named by Plotinus. The Plotinian image of polishing oneself into a beautiful statue (*statua speciosa*) is invoked as the precondition for prophetic vision.

---

## Technical Terms and Sources

- **Bat qol** (בת קול): Hebrew Kabbalistic term, "daughter of the voice" = all reflected forms of divine prophetic speech
- **Sympathia**: Greek term for occult cosmic correspondence, central to the natural-magical framework
- **Mantia**: Greek term for prophetic frenzy, etymologised (following Plato) from Manto, legendary founder of Mantua
- **Extasis / excessus mentis**: ecstatic states common to both Neoplatonic contemplatives and Christian mystics
- **Purgatoriae virtutes**: Plotinus's term (mediated by Porphyry) for the virtues that polish the soul for contemplation

Principal authorities cited in this opening portion: Hermes Trismegistus (*Poimandres*, *Asclepius*); Cicero (*De divinatione* I–II); Plato (*Ion*, *Phaedrus*, *Parmenides*); Aristotle (*Meteorologica*, *Nicomachean Ethics*, *Problemata*); Ptolemy (*Centiloquium*); Porphyry (*Life of Plotinus*); Proclus; Ovid (*Fasti*); Paul (Phil 4:13; John 15:5); Isaiah 41:23; 2 Peter 1:21; 1 Samuel 10:10; Job 37:7; Matthew 1:20; Ambrose; Aulus Gellius; Democritus; Rabbi Shem Tov ben Falaqera.

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section represents the culmination of the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s anthropological project: having established the harmonic structure of the cosmos through numerical, musical, and angelic hierarchies, Zorzi now articulates how man — as the microcosmic vinculum of all creation — can *act* within and upon that cosmic order. The capacity for prophecy and ecstasy is not an exceptional gift but the natural consequence of man's constitution as image of both God and world. The integration of Kabbalistic (*bat qol*), Neoplatonic (Plotinus, Proclus, Porphyry), Hermetic, and Christian mystical testimony under a single framework is exemplary of Zorzi's Christian Kabbalah: all genuine spiritual traditions converge on the same truth of divine indwelling and restored dominion.
